title: Missing Person Mom
theme: Some homes hold families. Some hold motive.
omen: a nursery monitor catching breathing nobody can identify

starter_premise.title: Missing Person Mom
starter_premise.premise: A child goes missing, mother knows something is off, and everybody ignores her for seventy minutes.
starter_premise.signature_tell: The county telegraphs the problem once and nobody listens until act three.

cast.name: Luke Mercer
traits: flinches at charm delivered too smoothly
can identify gaslighting by sentence three
still apologizes before telling the truth
secret_capacity: kept every voicemail because no one believed her the first time
cast.regional_responsibility: has keys to a church annex
cast.space_description: marina tackle room described as just storage

cast.name: Noah Hale
traits: weaponizes therapy vocabulary
says he has changed with suspicious fluency
texts at impossible hours
secret_capacity: has already charmed the sheriff before the story begins
cast.regional_responsibility: serves on a board no one fully understands
cast.space_description: dock office with a desk nobody fully claims
cast.suspicion_profile: knows where a secret could fit architecturally

cast.name: Elias Pike

keeps throat lozenges in a bag that feels doctrinally wrong
asks for passwords with fake embarrassment
secret_capacity: knows how to mimic the protagonist's voicemail tone
has already copied one signature for practice
cast.regional_responsibility: has keys to a church annex

secluded_space_access.space_type: side room with a space heater

cast.space_description: side room with a space heater and old family records
cast.suspicion_profile: appears trustworthy under pendant lighting only

cast.name: Rachel Marlowe
traits: wins over children by the second breakfast
arrives with glowing references and impossible calm
wears the mother's robe as if testing custody
secret_capacity: already knows where the family documents are kept
cast.regional_responsibility: organizes a fundraiser with tense undertones
cast.space_description: dock office with a desk nobody fully claims
cast.suspicion_profile: knows where a secret could fit architecturally

cast.name: Cole Dane
traits: mistakes refusal for intrigue
knows routines he should not know
says forever too early
secret_capacity: keeps a duplicate garage opener from a better time
cast.regional_responsibility: is trusted to lock up after emotionally charged events
cast.space_description: meditation annex nobody uses in winter
cast.suspicion_profile: can host Thanksgiving but could move a body-shaped rug

cast.name: Sienna Bell
traits: often wrong but directionally vital
says "I know what I saw"
always investigating
secret_capacity: accused an innocent scamp and feels sick about it
cast.regional_responsibility: organizes a fundraiser with tense undertones
cast.suspicion_profile: appears trustworthy under pendant lighting only

traits: distrusts every man immediately
emotionally precise
notices timeline errors before adults do

cast.name: Derek Cross
traits: knows the old case too well
definitely has rain-scene potential
hovers between competence and grief

beat_sheet.text: The engine is information asymmetry from the start: A child goes missing, mother knows something is off, and everybody ignores her for seventy minutes. Somebody already knows more than Luke Mercer, and the county intends to profit from that gap. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Cole Dane hesitates before explaining meditation annex nobody uses in winter. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.00.

Jenna Bennett or another sharp witness notices the first asymmetry beat before the adults do, usually through behavior that looks small until it becomes doctrine. Noah Hale hesitates before explaining dock office with a desk nobody fully claims. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.07. A basement light switches on in an empty house.

A social or domestic scene introduces the boat key alongside one piece of information the audience cannot yet correctly rank. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.14.

Rachel Marlowe offers a warning that sounds melodramatic until later when it becomes embarrassingly precise. Signature tell: The county telegraphs the problem once and nobody listens until act three. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Elias Pike hesitates before explaining side room with a space heater and old family records. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.23. The sheriff sees fresh mud on the boat key.

Act one ends with the gap established clearly enough to create pursuit but not enough to settle blame. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.31.

Luke Mercer starts catching up through snooping, searches, or one bad conversation, but every new answer creates a fresh asymmetry somewhere else. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.40.

The police or authority beat shows who the system currently believes, which is usually not the person doing the most correct thinking. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.50. Someone who should be dead appears at brunch.

An object or scene near dock office with a desk nobody fully claims weaponizes the knowledge gap by framing the wrong person or burying the right clue in plain sight. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.61.

The ally beat is really about information custody: who now holds the dangerous version of events and whether they can survive holding it. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.72. A phone vibrates from inside the wall.

Act two ends with Luke Mercer close enough to the truth to be dangerous but still one beat behind the person who shaped the narrative first. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Elias Pike hesitates before explaining side room with a space heater and old family records. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.84.

The reveal clarifies not just what happened but who benefited from everyone misreading the information gap in the first place. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Sienna Bell hesitates before explaining toolshed described as seasonal materials. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.97.

Jenna Bennett or the unofficial witness spots the timeline error, digital slip, or spatial contradiction that official people failed to honor. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.10. The sheriff sees fresh mud on the boat key.

A chase, drive, or locked-room pivot near Mercer Fertility and Sleep Clinic forces the remaining information into motion instead of theory. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.25.

The confrontation settles the visible gap, but the emotional cost depends on how much truth arrived too late. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.41. The ceramic owl is back on the kitchen island.

The ending decides whether the asymmetry closes cleanly or leaves one last opening in the county's memory. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.58. A phone vibrates from inside the wall.

contradiction_ledger.entry: Noah Hale is called Daniel in one kitchen scene and Derek during the rain argument.
Luke Mercer says Bellweather is in Vermont, then calls it outside Atlanta after two glasses of wine.
A dead best friend appears at brunch and nobody agrees whether this is denial, evil, or scheduling.
The basement belongs to a condo, a lake house, and an abandoned vineyard at different times in Alder Ridge.

dialogue_samples: "You think because you wear flannel and own a dock light I am supposed to trust you, Noah?"
"Mothers do not disappear, Jenna. They evaporate into paperwork."
"I checked his social media likes, Luke. Nobody who enjoys motivational quotes is innocent."
"The boat key is not decorative anymore and you know it."
"Trust is just fear with a casserole dish, Rachel."
"You seem upset," he said, making tea in a way that felt prosecutable.
"If that basement is innocent, why does it sound defensive?"
"I did not lie. I just told the truth in the order this town could survive."
"Honey, ever since the accident, your anxiety has been so high. Did you take your pills?"
"That pool was supposed to make us look safe, and somehow it became a witness."
"He keeps calling it stress like stress knows my garage code."
"If she is the nanny, why is she wearing my robe like a legal argument?"
fake_critic_blurb: "Missing Person Mom understands that menace is a regional utility." - County Suspense Quarterly
sequel_hook: Channel 8 Night Report hints that the vineyard fire had a witness who still hosts brunch.
synopsis: Inspired by true events in the emotional sense only, "Missing Person Mom" follows Luke Mercer, a recovery protagonist with paperwork caught inside widow maybe lying maybe surviving before she understood where the gap had already opened. Noah Hale collides with her life carrying portable suspicion, plausible access to offsite storage, and the kind of calm that sounds premeditated in a dim kitchen. The governing injury is simple: A child goes missing, mother knows something is off, and everybody ignores her for seventy minutes. The signature tell is always this: The county telegraphs the problem once and nobody listens until act three. As a nursery monitor catching breathing nobody can identify, what she cannot yet see becomes the syntax of the whole county, and every withheld fact keeps breathing just ahead of her. Inspired by something, probably.
